coredata.dc:title: UV-irradiated hydrothermal synthesis of reduced graphene quantum dots for electrochemical applications
coredata.dc:creator: Veeresh, S.
Ganesh, H.
Nagaraju, Y.S.
Vandana, M.
Ashokkumar, S.P.
Vijeth, H.
Prasad, M.V.N. Ambika
Devendrappa, H.
coredata.dc:description: This paper reports the structural and electrochemical properties of graphene quantum dots (GQDs) and reduced graphene quantum dots (rGQDs). The synthesis of GQDs by glucose and obtained the rGQDs by 365 nm UV-source irradiated glucose precursor used in microwave assisted hydrothermal reaction. The modification in chemical interaction was studied by Fourier Transforms Infrared (FT-IR) spectroscopy, and X-ray diffraction (XRD) confirms the structural change due to UV-irradiation effects. The Raman spectra confirms change in the intensity ratio of G and D band, and UV–Visible absorption peak of GQDs observed at 243, 245, 248 and 262 nm for 24, 48, and 72 h UV-irradiated modified rGQDs. Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) images shows reduced the size with increasing UV-irradiation time which confirms the rGQDs. Atomic force microscopy (AFM) employed to know topological surface study. The electrochemical performance of Cyclic Voltammetry from a typical rectangular shape of the integral area suggesting it is exhibits strong electrochemical activity and specific capacity behaviour by redox reaction at different scan rate from 100 to 500 mV/s for GQDs and rGQDs. The current-voltage plot exhibited a gradual increase in current with applied voltage as well as irradiation at different hours.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Graphene
Reduced graphene
Quantum dots
Cyclic voltammetry
Current-voltage

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Synthesis of reduced Graphene quantum dots

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: The author would like to thank Mangalore University for providing research fellowship.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Probably chemical representation in (a) GQDs and (b) 365 nm UV irradiated modified rGQDs.
FT-IR Spectra of GQDs, UV-Source 24, 48 and 72 h irradiated modified rGDQs.
XRD patterns of GQDs, 365 nm UV-source 24, 48 and 72 h modified rGQDs.
Raman spectra of GQDs, UV source 24, 48 and 72 h irradiated modified rGQDs.
UV–visible spectroscopy of GQDs, UV source 24, 48 and 72 h irradiated modified rGQDs.
PL emission spectra of GQDs, UV-source 24, 48 and 72 h irradiated modified rGQDs.
TEM image of (a) GQDs and the corresponding histograms of their Gaussian size distribution. (b) 24, (c) 48 and (d) 72 h UV-irradiated modified rGQDs.

AFM image of GQDs, 24, 48, and 72 h rGQDs respectively. (a, c, e, g) size and height distribution. High profile along the indicate of GQDs, 24, 48, 72 h rGQDs (b, d, f, h) UV-irradiated modified rGQDs.

Cyclic Voltammetry curve of (a) GQDs, (b) 24, (c) 48 and (d) 72 h UV-irradiated modified rGQDs, (e) variation of specific capacitance (Csp) of GQDs electrode and (f) 72 h irradiated electrode at different scan rates calculated by CV curve.
I-V characteristics of (a) GQDs, (b) 24, (c) 48 and (d) 72 h UV-irradiated modified rGQDs.

head.ce:title: UV-irradiated hydrothermal synthesis of reduced graphene quantum dots for electrochemical applications

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Physics, Mangalore University, Mangalagangothri 574199, India
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Physics, Mangalore University, Mangalagangothri-574199, India

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Physics, Mangalore Institute of Technology and Engineering, Moodbidri 574225, India

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Mangalore Institute of Technology and Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Physics, Gulbarga University, Kalburgi-585106, India

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Physics, Gulbarga University, Kalburgi 585106, India
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Physics, Gulbarga University, Kalburgi-585106, India

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: This paper reports the structural and electrochemical properties of graphene quantum dots (GQDs) and reduced graphene quantum dots (rGQDs). The synthesis of GQDs by glucose and obtained the rGQDs by 365 nm UV-source irradiated glucose precursor used in microwave assisted hydrothermal reaction. The modification in chemical interaction was studied by Fourier Transforms Infrared (FT-IR) spectroscopy, and X-ray diffraction (XRD) confirms the structural change due to UV-irradiation effects. The Raman spectra confirms change in the intensity ratio of G and D band, and UV–Visible absorption peak of GQDs observed at 243, 245, 248 and 262 nm for 24, 48, and 72 h UV-irradiated modified rGQDs. Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) images shows reduced the size with increasing UV-irradiation time which confirms the rGQDs. Atomic force microscopy (AFM) employed to know topological surface study. The electrochemical performance of Cyclic Voltammetry from a typical rectangular shape of the integral area suggesting it is exhibits strong electrochemical activity and specific capacity behaviour by redox reaction at different scan rate from 100 to 500 mV/s for GQDs and rGQDs. The current-voltage plot exhibited a gradual increase in current with applied voltage as well as irradiation at different hours.

ce:section.ce:para: Graphene based single planar sheet of sp2-bonded carbon atoms have been emerged as a new group graphene quantum was confined semiconductor in recent years because of its exceptional unique morphology, photo luminescent, large surface area, electrochemical behaviour, and low toxicity whenever creates the defect in graphene. It has zero optical band gap may cause the no photoluminescence (PL) behaviours could be the main reason why this material is unable to use in the opto-electronic applications. Therefore, it would be very much essential to opening the band gap by introduce defects in the graphene structure, in which creation of intermediate energy level is an advantage for fast charge transition between valance band to conduction band. The substitute the oxygen, hydroxyl and carboxyl groups into graphene as a result the interaction with functional group of basal planes of graphene structure or evolution of hydrogen may create the defect on the surface of GO, which enhanced the surface charge density or surface to volume charge ratios leads to improve favourable PL and redox behaviour. Graphene quantum dots (GQDs), and reduced graphene quantum dots (rGQDs) are the fragments of graphene comprising single or few layers of graphene with size in the range of 3–10 nm [2]. The chemistry of GQDs, and rGQDs are layer with hydroxyl (OH), carbonyl (C=O), carboxylic acid (=COOH) and other oxygen functional groups. These free radical functional groups are playing a vital role in these materials whenever they undergo chemical interact with other materials [3–5] and directly provide the energy through UV-irradiation [6].

The breaking of hydrogen from OH functional group of graphene oxide whenever direct energy applied through UV-irradiated as a result modification in their size, and surface would be providing the outstanding photoluminescence (PL) and electrochemical properties [7] and it is clearly representing in the Fig. 1 . If glucose irradiated by 365 nm Ultraviolet source and used the hydrothermal synthesis to obtain the solution of rGQDs with significant features like structure and size correspondingly change their band gap also [8]. There are few reported results evidenced that change in the band of GQDs [9,10], have been proved that superior photo stability [11], and wide range of fluorescence [12]. Over the past decades, two-photon fluorescent (TPF) probes have been extensively developed due to their low background interference and weak photo bleaching [13]. In addition, through the rGQDs active channel voltage hysteresis and gate program, erase and reprogram biases. First structure of the rGQDs was constructed to examine memory charging effect feature in hysteretic C − V behaviour of cross-linked poly vinyl alcohol (PVA) [14,15]. The rGQDs in a solution show blue emissions at 395 nm under excitation with a 325 nm HeCd laser, the adjusted PL spectra also exhibited wavelength values between 385 and 460 nm with a xenon lamp. The relative insensitivity of PL position depending on rGQDs size and the effect of oxidation or defect states on PL intensity will provide solid ground for the understanding of graphene photonics [16]. In general, photo catalysis mainly comprise three steps: first, with irradiation of 365 nm UV-light or visible light the electrons from the valence band (VB) realize the transition into the conduction band (CB), which forms an equal number of holes at the original location; second is the photo excited charges (electrons and holes) migrate to the surface of semiconductor; and third is the electrons and holes act as reduction and oxidant to realize dye degradation and water splitting [17]. It depends on the oxidation and reduction by reacting with working electrode or generated active radical's electrons. These active radicals are produced by ultraviolet (UV) irradiation acted to accelerate the generation of rGQDs and regulate their degree of surface oxidation [2]. And these change in the chemistry due to breaking of hydrogen from GO structure. Application of colloidal quantum dots (QDs) in the form of a film layer has been proposed with a quantum size effect, in which fabricated in the semiconductor nanostructure. For example, quantum dot field effect transistors (QD-FETs), LEDs, and various sensors have been demonstrated with semiconducting quantum effects [18]. Recently, graphene-based semiconductor nanoparticles (NPs) also termed rGQDs they have attracted more attention as a new type of luminescent NPs because of their remarkable electrical conductivity and electrochemical properties are suitable to use in various potential applications such as photo catalysis, solar cell, biosensor, bio imaging, and opto-electronic properties [19]. From literature review, it is reported that the various sized rGQDs with different photo luminescent colors ranging from the visible to near-infrared region [20]. Interestingly, it is observed that yield of rGQDs under white light is much significant than that from GQDs and it demonstrate the reduction of rGQDs with hydrazine hydrate found to be decreasing the band gap which attributes to improved inter-intra band electron transition and fluorescence properties of rGQDs [21]. It depends upon the quantum confinement, edge effect, surface passivation and also on the synthesis method. So, it can be improving the emission color by controlling the irradiation time and the precursors used [22,23]. It can also interpret that the PL is associated with the π* → n transition of electrons and observed quantum confinement is ascribed to the change the size of sp2 domains, which leads to a change in the π-π* energy gap; the n-state levels remain unaffected by the change in size [3]. Although optical and structural properties of rGQDs have been studied extensively, and also describing the effects of 365 nm UV-irradiation or high-pressure mercury lamp providing only a partial insight change their physical and chemistry of the rGQDs. This UV-irradiated modified rGQDs has utilizing for the purpose of UV detection on the basis of UV-induced optical changes and other sensing property. The optical response improved rGQDs is successfully utilized as a photocurrent generation layer in a novel UV-irradiation [24]. However, for instance the rGQDs prepared from glucose via acidic oxidation were functionalized with hydroxyl, carbonyl, and carboxylic acid containing oxygen groups.

Various chemical and physical techniques have been reported for the synthesis of glucose particles. The chemical techniques have certain limitation including strict reaction protocols, toxic reagents, time consumption and unstable particles. The synthesis of glucose particles, with reduced size and better shape, through direct physical techniques. The graphene quantum dots were prepared via hydrothermal method by using glucose powder showed the formation of glucose particles was confirmed by the yellow color solution of the GQDs as the precursor in an alkaline environment.

In addition, it is also used microwave radiation, gamma radiation or ultraviolet (UV) irradiated glucose for synthesis the rGQDs. There is very less amount of work reported on UV irradiated glucose (reduced and copping agent) used for synthesis of rGQDs. Thus, the present study was planned to use UV-irradiation exposed to glucose as a physical technique, and for the hydrothermal synthesis for rGQDs. The impact of increasing time of UV- irradiation from 24, 48 and 72 h on the synthesis in the solution form rGQDs, size and distribution of particles was also studies.

In the present study, glucose particles were synthesized by UV-irradiated successfully and glucose was used as “stabilizer”. Further, the impact of irradiation time on the formation of particles along with the size particles was also considered. Glucose solution along with 0.30 M glucose aqueous solution showed the formation of glucose particles was confirmed by the dark color solution. After 72 h of UV-irradiated, the formation of particles was due to the reduction of glucose, changing the color of clear colloidal solution into darker gray color solution. During the reduction process, the produced glucose particles were attracted by the hydroxyl (OH) ends of malto-triose units of glucose. As a result, glucose acts as a strong protective layer and a highly functional coating for glucose particles, with better stability in the solution over time [26,27].

Here, synthesis of rGQDs through hydrothermal method by using 365 nm UV-source irradiated glucose at different hours used as a precursor in this synthesis of solution form. These rGQDs exhibits excellent photoluminescence (PL) behaviour and good water dispensability. The size dependence of the PL result from quantum confinements in the sp2 domain is elucidated in the following discussions. Several spectroscopic (UV–Vis, FT-IR, Raman and XRD) TEM and AFM techniques are employed for their characterization and analysis. Electrochemical technique cyclic voltammetry (CV), specific capacitance at scan rate and electrochemical linear sweep voltammetry are used for the studies the electrochemical performance. The experimental studies have proved that the rGQDs is a prominent material for electrochemical application.
Glucose (99%) (C6H12O6) bulk powder was purchased from Sigma Aldrich and polyvinyl alcohol (PVA) from Merck Millipore, India.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of reduced Graphene quantum dots
ce:section.ce:para: UV irradiated microwave assisted hydrothermal method was used to synthesis the reduced graphene quantum dots (rGQDs) using 365 nm Ultraviolet source different hours irradiated glucose used as a precursor. The glucose exposed by 365 nm UV-source at different 24, 48 and 72 h, further 6 g of irradiated glucose powder was dissolved in 50 ml of distilled water and magnetic stirred about 30 min for complete dissolution. The solution was placed in stainless steel autoclave with a Teflon liner 100 ml capacity was heated at 180 °C for 4 h. In this experiment it is noticed that the heating time, microwave power, source concentration, and pressure parameters are affected on the growth of rGQDs and its size. After completion of experimental process, the sample solution form undergoes color change from reddish yellow color solution to dark gray color solution indicates formation of reduced graphene quantum dots (rGDQs) solution and it was used for further characterizations. Similar method has been followed for different hours irradiated glucose. Fig. 1 represents the synthesis steps of GQDs and irradiated modified solution form rGQDs.

The Fig. 1, illustrates the probably chemical representation in GQDs and 365 nm UV-source irradiated modified solution form rGQDs. TEM image reveal that the shape and size reduced for 72 h irradiated corresponds the reduction of hydrogen from GO structure this modification is due to the UV-irradiation effect.

The chemical change was examined by Fourier transform infrared spectrometer (FT-IR, model ALPHA BURKUE) in the range 4000–400 cm−1 and Powder X-ray diffraction (XRD) pattern have recorded using Rigaku Miniflex 500 diffractometer Cu Kα radiation in the range of 5-70o/min with scan rate 5o/min per minute to know the material phase. A Raman spectrum was examined from Raman spectroscopy using high resolution spectrometer (Horiba, Lab Ram HR) with a 514 laser beam. The optical absorbance was measured as a function of wavelength (λ) in the range from 190 to 1100 nm with the help of computerized double beam Perkin-Elmer Lambda-35 UV–visible spectrophotometer. Photoluminescence (PL) spectra of aqueous solution of GQDs and rGQDs were recorded with help of Agilent Carry Eclipse Fluorescence Spectrophotometer. Transmission Electron Microscope (TEM) image of the sample was obtained by using a JEOL JEM-2100F model. The AFM images were measured with SPA400 (Seiko instrument Inc.) on new cleaved mica surface in tapping mode in air. The CHI 660E electrochemical workstation is use to record cyclic voltammetry and current-voltage parameters.

FT-IR spectroscopy was used to examine the presence of chemical change in the GQDs and UV-source irradiated modified rGQDs as shown in Fig. 2 . The GQDs broad absorbance observed at 1662 cm−1 is assigned to the stretching vibration of CC and 1525 cm−1 corresponding to the CC bond stretching vibration.

The broad absorption bands at 2350 and 3725 cm−1 are attributed the COOH and –OH bonds. In case of 24 h irradiated rGQDs an aromatic CC stretching peak is observed at 1687 cm−1 is assigned the vibration of carbonyl group [23,28]. For 48 h irradiated modified rGQDs shows peaks at 1525 and 1700 cm−1 associated with the vibration of CC and COOH bands respectively [21,29] and broad band 2362 and 3737 cm−1 are related to the absorption band of stretching vibration of CO and –OH group. In case of 72 h rGQDs band were observed at 1531 cm−1 is attributed the stretching vibration of CC and CO band bending peak at 1712 cm−1. An absorption band at 2375 cm−1 is also absorbed. This band deformation can be attributed to the CH and 3750 cm−1 is due to the -OH stretching vibration of hydroxyl group. The spectral results described above reflected that rGQDs have many oxygenated functional groups such as carboxylic acid, alkoxy, aldehydes and carbonyl group on their surfaces [30–33]. The peaks have shift towards higher wave number due to the increasing the irradiation hours.

The XRD pattern of GQDs and UV-irradiated modified rGQDs are shown in Fig. 3 . XRD pattern of GQDs exhibit sharp peak at 2θ = 9.5° and 2θ = 20.25° corresponding to the d-spacing of 0.47 nm and 0.53 nm. XRD peaks observed at 2θ = 23.21° and 2θ = 28.6° are confirm the crystalline nature of GQDs [6,34,35]. After 24 h irradiated modified rGQDs exhibit broad peak at 2θ = 20.8° with decreasing interlayer spacing of 0.28 nm [36].

The crystallite size can be calculated using Debye-Scherer's relation, which is given by
D =
kλ βcosθ
where D represents the size of the crystallite, the wavelength of the X-ray radiation used is given by λ, θ denotes Bragg's angle, and β is (FWHM) full-width at half maximum in radian.

The 48 h irradiated modified rGQDs exhibit a broad peak at 2θ = 20.87°, additionally this broad line in the amorphous nature peak shifting to a higher side 2θ = 24.9° and 2θ = 29.3° corresponding interlayer d-spacing of 1 nm, 0.29 nm, 0.53 nm and 0.16 nm [37,38].

The crystalline nature along with the synthesized after 72 h irradiate modified rGQDs was further confirmed by XRD. It can be observed that the sharp peak in the crystalline nature. The particle exhibited three peaks in XRD analysis at 2 theta/degree at 2θ = 9.2°, 2θ = 20.12° and 2θ = 28.48° corresponding to an interlayer d-spacing of 0.82, 0.72, and 0.19 nm respectively. The intensities of the reflection due to the rGQDs phase were also found to increase along with the increased different hours rGQDs content in the UV-irradiation [38,40,41].

Raman spectroscopy also uses to characterize the GQDs and UV-irradiated modified rGQDs as shown in Fig. 4 . There are two characteristics bands in the Raman spectra of carbon-based material relative intensity of D and G bands (ID/IG) was estimated. The former is related to sp3 hybridized carbon atoms to the presence of structural defect, while the latter it is associated with sp2 hybridized carbon atoms.

The crystalline D band at 2898 cm−1 and amorphous G band at 3210 cm−1, with intensity ratio of ID/IG as produced GQDs was only around 2.3, are ascribed to ordered sp2 bonded carbon and disordered carbon, respectively, are found to be broadened due to the high disorder and random orientation of GQDs with respect to each other. After UV-irradiated the crystalline and amorphous of D and G band in 24 h modified irradiated rGQDs were observed at 2886 cm−1 and at 3199 cm−1 with an intensity ratio of 0.2 respectively. For 48 h modified irradiated rGQDs it was found that peak position of D band at 1946 cm−1 and G band at 3141 cm−1 with intensity ratio ID/IG of 0.7 which was similar to that of solution. Observed that 72 h modified irradiated rGQDs hybrids present peaks at 2915 and 3192 cm−1 resulting from the typical D and G band respectively. Based on the empirical relations ID/IG intensity ratio of 0.1, which is much lower than that of higher irradiation hours. One can see that the G band is dominate for GQDs but the D band become stronger for rGQDs, which indicate that more defects are formed in rGQDs during glucose reduction. Which signify the D and G bands are found deceasing order confirmed the irradiation effect [42–47].

The study of UV–Visible absorption of GQDs and UV-irradiated modified rGQDs in the aqueous solution form is shown in Fig. 5 . The UV–vis spectrum of GQDs displays a typical absorption band at around ~243 nm which can be attributed to the π-π* transition of aromatic CC sp2 domains [49]. After UV-irradiated to shift slightly for 24 h irradiated modified rGQDs was absorption band at 245 nm is assigned to the π-π* transition of aromatic -C=C and –C-C- band in the sp2 hybridized domain of graphene core. The second absorption band at 345 nm is attributed to the n-π* transition of -C=O, -C-H band in the sp3 hybridized domain [50,51].

For 48 h irradiated rGQDs it is seen that optical band slightly increased absorption band shifted at 248 and 347 nm is assigned attributed to the n-π* transition of CO bands, respectively [52]. As they are 72 h modified irradiated rGQDs shows absorption bands at 262, 307 and 374 nm in the ultraviolet region are clearly visible for all three bands. After increased higher irradiation different hour, and there is occurrence of red shift with an increase in size confirmed the irradiation effect [53,54].

The photoluminescence (PL) of GQDs and UV-irradiated modified rGQDs as shown in Fig. 6 . The excitation wavelength of GQDs shows peak at 508 nm in the visible region [55], and PL emission spectra peak observed at 492 nm for 24 h irradiated modified rGQDs. The excitation PL has been attributed to different emitting species or the localization of electron- hole pair owing to the isolated sp2 cluster within sp3 matrix.

The PL emission spectra of 48 h irradiated rGQDs show a peak at 487 nm and the peak have shifted towards higher side monotonically with decreasing intensity. For 72 h irradiated modified rGQDs, show peak at 583 nm, and intensity peak found to be decreased rapidly, and increase as the excitation wavelength due to irradiation effect. It has been reported that the PL emission in rGQDs is dependent on the chemical nature of their surfaces. The hybridization carbon backbone and the carbonyl group at the edge have been demonstrated to be associated with the luminescence [58,59]. These observed results suggesting that UV irradiation effect is predominate in the material.

The transmission electron microscopy (TEM) of GQDs and UV-irradiated modified rGQDs as shown in Fig. 7 . The GQDs image shown nearly spherical shape with a relatively uniform size with diameter ranging 4–14 nm and its Gaussian size distribution plot inset inserted in Fig. 7(a) and it histogram shows an average size is ~9 nm with proper distribution curve clearly evidenced monodispersed and are much smaller than those of GQDs around (~3–10 nm).

The 24 h UV-irradiated modified rGQDs image show in Fig. 7(b) demonstrates high crystallinity structure with an interplanar lattice spacing of 1.36 nm. For 48 h reveals that higher crystallinity with a narrow size change and average particle size of 2.64 nm as shown in Fig. 7(c). For 72 h shows an average size of 5.08 nm and fictionalization shape and size with UV-irradiation exposure represented well defined diffraction pattern confirmed the glucose reduction due to the crystalline structure in the rGQDs as shown in Fig. 7(d) [60–63].

As shown in Fig. 8 . The topography of GQDs, and rGQDs were investigated by AFM. Fig. 8(a) illustrated the topography morphology and the height distribution of the GQDs. The high profile in Fig. 8(b) shows that the average thickness of GQDs is about ~38.5 nm and the height distribution is in the range of 0.8 nm. Obtained results suggesting that the produced GQDs and rGQDs consisted with 2–4 layers graphene [64,65].

The surface roughness factor of the films after UV-irradiated by AFM images as shown in Fig. 8(c, e, g). AFM images surface there is a change in size after short time UV irradiation. However, the height distribution for rGQDs under various UV-irradiated time clearly show that the height distribution of few layer rGQDs decrease from 0.6 to 0.25 nm is cited in Fig. 8(d, f, h) high profile average thickness from 32.3 to 24.6 nm after different hours UV-irradiated, indicating that the few layer rGQDs higher UV-irradiated have been exfoliated to monolayer ones [66]. The significant change in surface morphology with increases the different hours confirm that the reduced after UV-irradiated effect [50,67–69].

The cyclic voltammetry (CV) is used to examine the electrochemical performance of GQDs and UV-irradiated modified rGQDs in the potential windows from −3.3 to 1.4 V using three electrode systems. The platinum wire is used as a counter, Ag/AgCl as a reference and glassy carbon as a working electrode as shown in Fig. 9 . From the Fig. 9(a) it is seen that the shape of the CV curves remains same and the area under the CV curves increases with increasing different scan rate from 100 to 500 mV/s for rGQDs. The 24 h UV-irradiated modified rGQDs shows increasing the current as shown in Fig. 9(b), similarly Fig. 9(c) and (d) also shows that current increases gradually and a clearly narrow shift between oxidation and reduction peak owing to the fact that the more porous structure contributed to high specific capacitance [47,70–72].

The specific capacitance (Csp) was determined from the CV curve using the below equation.
C sp
=
∫
V 1
V 2
i
V
dV
V 1
−
V 2
v m
where ∫V1 V2 i(V) dV indicate the areas enclosed under the CV curve, V1 and V2 represent the lower and upper voltage limits respectively, the scan rate is given by v and m given the mass in g of active electrode material. In Fig. 9(e) it shows that the mass specific capacitance of the GQDs electrode is 240.47, 183.32, 156.90, 150.46 and 126.38 F/g at different sweep rates of 100, 200, 300, 400, and 500 mV/s respectively. This mass specific capacitance displays decreasing trend with increasing scan rate, it attributed to reduce the time of ion diffusion in complete cyclic mechanism. Moreover, in Fig. 9(f) the mass specific capacitance of the 72 h rGQDs electrode exhibited 258.49, 160.66, 123.7, 100.33and 87.20 F/g at different sweep rates of 100, 200, 300, 400 and 500 mV/s respectively. Obviously, the mass specific capacitance of the sample 72 h rGQDs is higher than that of GQDs, which can be owing to the larger surface area of GQDs leading to more active sites for contacting ions in the electrolyte [42,74,75].

The I-V characterization curve of the GQDs and UV-irradiated modified rGQDs as shown in Fig. 10 . The current increases with increasing applied voltage as well as the UV-irradiated hours in 0.1 M KOH electrolyte at different scan rate 100 to 500 mV/s.

The 24 and 48 h rGQDs, electro catalytic activity for the potential of oxidation/reduction is evaluated, the increase in current with increases applied voltage at different scan rate 100 to 500 m V/s respectively. An I-V curve current increase at 72 h rGQDs have much larger area confirm the rGQDs is the good current voltage stability and suitable for various potential applications [70,76–81].

In summary, highly luminescence reduced graphene quantum dots (rGQDs) have synthesized by 365 nm UV-source different hours irradiated glucose solution. The FT-IR resul [1,25,56] ts confirmed the chemical changed by breaking of OH and COOH groups with UV-irradiation effect. The XRD results confirm the decreasing amorphous phase by increasing crystallinity. Raman spectra confirmed presence of various oxygen containing functional groups, there are two characteristic band in Raman spectra of carbon-based materials crystalline D band and amorphous G band to defect intensity ratio. UV–Visible optical absorption found decreased and it exhibited red shift. The photoluminescence (PL) show excitation dependent luminescence behaviour from blue to red luminescence under irradiation different hours. The TEM results show that the average size distribution is 9 nm which confirms the synthesis of reduced graphene quantum dots. The AFM topological results clearly show that monolayer formed by aggregation of graphene. The cyclic voltammetry curve reveals that the graphene electrode is electrochemically active redox reaction at different scan rate 100 to 500 mV/s. The current-voltage characteristics curve increases with irradiation. This may favour these materials to be considered for electrochemical properties.

It is declared that results presented in the manuscript are not violates the research ethics and also assure all results presented in the manuscript done by Ph D student in our research laboratory. And these results are not submitted to any journal/reviews/others for publication. Therefore, I undersigned here again results are not violet ethic and values of research.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: It is declared that results presented in the manuscript are did not have any complicit interest. And also these results are not submitted to any journal/reviews/others for publication.

ce:section.ce:para: The author would like to thank Mangalore University for providing research fellowship.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Hydrothermal route for cutting graphene sheets into blue-luminescent graphene quantum dots
Adv. Mater.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: D. Pan, J. Zhang, Z. Li, M. Wu, Hydrothermal route for cutting graphene sheets into blue-luminescent graphene quantum dots, Adv. Mater. 22 (6) (2010) 734-738.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Free-radical-assisted rapid synthesis of graphene quantum dots and their oxidizability studies
Langmuir

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Y. Li, H. Liu, Liu, X. S. Q. Li, L. Wang, N. Ma, D. Qiu, Free-radical-assisted rapid synthesis of graphene quantum dots and their oxidizability studies, Langmuir 32(34) (2016) 8641-8649.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Elucidating quantum confinement in graphene oxide dots based on excitation-wavelength-independent photoluminescence
J. Phys. Chem. Lett.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: T. F. Yeh, W. L. Huang, C. J. Chung, I. T. Chiang, L. C. Chen, H. Y. Chang, H. Teng, Elucidating quantum confinement in graphene oxide dots based on excitation-wavelength-independent photoluminescence, J Phys. Chem. Lett. 7(11) (2016) 2087-2092.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Carbon quantum dot-modified carbon paste electrode-based sensor for selective and sensitive determination of adrenaline
ACS omega

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: S. S. Shankar, R. M. Shereema, V. Ramachandran, T. V. Sruthi, V. S. Kumar, R. B. Rakhi, Carbon Quantum Dot-Modified Carbon Paste Electrode-Based Sensor for Selective and Sensitive Determination of Adrenaline, ACS omega 4(4)(2019) 7903-7910.
